channel.title: Informed Consent on Riad Dabboussi | Business & Data Analytics Professional
channel.description: Recent content in Informed Consent on Riad Dabboussi | Business & Data Analytics Professional

item.title: When Anonymous Isn't Really Anonymous: The Ethics Behind Student Course Surveys
item.description:
Introduction

Feedback plays an important role in improving education systems. Universities and colleges often rely on student surveys at the end of a course to understand what worked well and what needs improvement. These surveys are used by decision-makers to improve teaching quality, update course content, and enhance the overall student experience. In this scenario, students are asked to complete a course evaluation survey through an online system. To access the survey, they must log in using their personal username and password. At the same time, the survey states that responses are anonymous and will only be used to improve courses. At first glance, this may seem like a standard and harmless process. However, when examined more closely, it raises important ethical questions about privacy, transparency, trust, and the responsible use of data. This blog explores the ethical concerns in the survey design, identifies additional risks, suggests improvements, and explains how a data analyst should respond if ethical recommendations are ignored.
